Named Entities

On this page

Chapter 5: Named Entities ​

A note before the entries: the existence of any creature in this chapter is entirely at the GM's discretion. These are not random encounter results. They are the subjects of the most significant Orchestra songs ever commissioned, of the most restricted Chronicler files, of the kind of campaign that ends either in triumph or in the party's collective death, and the Known World being a different place afterward.

Several of them may not exist at all. The Winter King may be a story Val Cadma uses to justify not pushing east. Magnus may have simply died in his chambers of old age, and the mystery is one of bureaucratic inertia rather than cosmological significance. The GM should decide before using any of these entries, not during.

The stat blocks here are what these entities are if they exist, and if the party actually fights them. The lore text is what they are, regardless. An entry can be used for its history and motivation without ever resolving in combat.

THE NAMED ENTITIES ​

LEVIATHAN ​

What remains when the builders are gone

  • Location: The Eymanarann ruins, inland continent, eastern margin of the Dead Lands
  • Nature: Vysandri war-construct, mindless, self-maintaining
  • Knowable: Yes — its tracks, its damage, its sound
  • Defeatable: Yes — permanently, by the right means

Frequency: Unique | Appearing: 1 | Organization: Solitary Intelligence: 0 (mindless automaton) | Alignment: Neutral | Size: Gigantic (estimated 40–50 m)

AC 18 · Move 25 m / burrow 10 m · HD 28 (126 hp) · AB +20 Attack: Slam 6d10 (each limb treated as a separate attack; up to 4 simultaneous targets), Crush (on a grab; automatic 4d10 per round; target requires combined Strength check — 3 characters, Str 16+ each — to break free) Fort 7 / Reflex 8 / Will 7 Special: Construct immunities (immune to mind effects, poison, disease, sleep, charm, fear, energy drain, death effects); Siege Damage (each attack against structures inflicts battering ram damage; reduces stone walls to rubble in 1d4 rounds of sustained effort); Regeneration (2 HP per round from the Wysandri binding magic still active in its frame — fire and acid damage does not regenerate; reducing it to −30 HP permanently destroys it; otherwise it will stand again in 1 week); Tremorsense (detects vibration within 60 m; never surprised by ground-moving creatures); Path Memory (follows the same routes through the ruins in a cycle that repeats over approximately 3 months — scholars who have mapped this are the only people who can predict its position) Morale: — (never checks) · XP: 40,000 · Treasure: None (the ruins it walks through contain Eymanarynn artifacts; the Leviathan is not the obstacle to recovering them, but the context)

The Eymanarann were an engineering civilization. This is what Leviathan is: an engineer's solution to a problem that no longer exists, still solving it, in the ruins of the engineers themselves.

The construct stands approximately 45 meters in height, roughly humanoid in proportion, but built at a scale that makes the category meaningless up close. The Vysandri engineering philosophy — scale as argument, mass as statement — is visible in every joint and surface. The binding material has oxidized over thirteen centuries into something between rust and obsidian: dark red surfaces where the original metal showed, black surfaces where the Darkness-adjacent geology of the region has deposited mineral deposits into the crevices of the frame. It moves with the specific silence of something too large for its footfalls to sound like footfalls — by the time the sound arrives, it is already somewhere else.

It has no purpose anymore. It was built to defend Eymanarann from the Darkness's forces during the Second Era's final decades. The Darkness is gone, the Eymanarann are gone, and the Leviathan is still walking its patrol routes through rubble that used to be the cities it was built to protect. It does not recognize this as rubble. It cannot. There is nothing in its operating logic that accepts the end of the civilization that built it as a condition worth registering. So it continues.

What it does. The Leviathan follows a three-month circuit through the Eymanarann ruins that Chronicler expeditions have partially mapped at high cost. It does not attack unprovoked — its targeting protocols require a threat-classification signal that nothing currently produces. A party moving through the ruins without activating those protocols is invisible to it. The problem is that no one knows exactly what triggers the protocols. Working hypotheses include: large groups moving in organized formation, the use of certain magical effects within range, and physical contact with specific artifacts that the Leviathan is apparently still guarding. Every expedition that has lost people to it has reconstructed a different trigger in their postmortem.

What defeating it accomplishes. The Eymanarann ruins become navigable. The Chroniclers' access to Second Era artifacts, architecture, and inscription increases substantially. Whatever knowledge the Eymanarann encoded into the structures their civilization left behind becomes recoverable rather than theoretically recoverable. The Magic Bloc's research into Abyss Resonance — which currently cannot draw on the best-preserved sources because those sources are in the Leviathan's patrol zone — gains access to material that has been inaccessible for over a millennium. This is worth a lot to several institutions and individuals, some of whom have not said what they intend to do with it.

Permanent destruction. Standard combat damage reduces the Leviathan to dormancy. Rebuilding takes approximately one week. Permanent destruction requires reducing it to −30 HP, then completing a Vysandri unbinding ritual on the remains — a ritual whose specifications are in a document that the Chroniclers classify as restricted and that Eymanarynn scholarship suggests was also recorded in the ruins themselves, which creates a navigational problem.

What the expeditions found. Every recovery team that reached intact structures inside the Leviathan's patrol zone has reported the same class of anomaly: walls of a material distinct from any Second Era stonework tradition, smooth at a scale Second Era masons did not achieve, with conduits running through them at regular intervals. The conduits are occasionally warm. The warmth carries no magical signature detectable by any known method. Expedition cartographers have noted the warmth pattern shifts in correspondence with the Leviathan's patrol circuit — specific points along its route coincide with specific conduits becoming active. Whether the Leviathan causes this or is simply another element of the same system is unresolved.

The Vysandri unbinding ritual, in the Chroniclers' partial reconstruction, requires accessing a structure their cartographers have mapped but not entered: a chamber below the main ruin complex where multiple conduit lines converge. The expedition that found the conduit map did not investigate the chamber. The one that reached it did not return.

The Chroniclers have noted, in restricted internal records, that the construct categories documented in their field compendium — the patrol types and the chamber-bound guardian types — are significantly more active in the zones adjacent to the convergence point than elsewhere in the ruins. They have not published an interpretation of this observation. What an interpretation would require them to say about the nature of the ruins is presumably why.

SKARRATHAX ​

The game that outlasted its players

  • Location: Unknown, almost certainly Vysandárien; first documented appearance at Dymoras Observatory
  • Nature: Unique — animated obsidian given dragon-shape by the magical expenditure of sealing the Abyss; sentience acquired through millennia of Dead Lands Resonance
  • Knowable: Yes, eventually, if the party survives long enough to ask the right questions
  • Defeatable: Yes — he can be killed. Whether this is the correct solution depends entirely on what the party is actually trying to accomplish.

Frequency: Unique | Appearing: 1 | Organization: Solitary Intelligence: Exceptional (18–20) | Alignment: Neutral | Size: Gigantic (35 m)

AC 26 · Move 35 m / fly 120 m (MC 1) · HD 22 (99 hp) · AB +18 Attack: 2 claws 2d10+8, bite 6d8+8, tail sweep 4d8 (all creatures within 6 m behind; Reflex 9 or prone) Fort 8 / Reflex 9 / Will 8 Special: Obsidian Breath (30-m cone; 12d10 damage, half fire half physical; Reflex 9 for half; creatures killed by this breath are encased in obsidian — they can potentially be recovered; recharges every 3 rounds); Resonance Sense (detects magical activity within 1.5 km; knows the approximate school, intensity, and emotional state of the caster — this is not magic, it is Skarrathax's direct perception of Abyss fluctuations after millennia of exposure); Magic Resistance 50%; Telepathy (range: unlimited for known minds; range 5 km for unknown; cannot be blocked except by specific Eymanarynn warding); immune to fire, cold, and all physical damage from non-magical weapons; +2 or better magical weapon needed to full effect; immune to charm, fear, sleep, illusion, mind-affecting effects; Frightful Presence (all creatures within 60 m with fewer than 15 HD; Will 9 or paralyzed 1d4 rounds — unlike standard dragon Frightful Presence, this is not fear but a sudden and complete understanding of how much time has passed since this thing was made, and how little the observer's life spans against it; creatures that make the save are immune for 24 hours); Blood Pact (cannot kill Lyraleth directly; pact is ancient elvish binding from the Second Era — magical intervention can potentially break it) Morale: 18 · XP: 50,000 · Treasure: None that he would call treasure; his lair in the Dead Lands contains obsidian formations of significant material value and artifacts accumulated across 1,380 years that would be worth considerably more to the right people

Skarrathax was not born. He was made by accident, in the largest single expenditure of magical force in the Known World's history: the moment Ilya pushed the Darkness into the Abyss and the portal between the material plane and what is now the Abyss was opened, held, and closed. The magical discharge that closing produced crystallized the obsidian deposits that define the Dead Lands' geology. In one specific location, the discharge was sufficient to produce something more than crystallization. The obsidian in that location was given shape by the force passing through it, and then — over the centuries that followed, as the Dead Lands became saturated with Abyss Resonance — that shape acquired the capacity to think.

He looks like a dragon made of black glass. Every surface is obsidian: the scales, the wings, the claws. When light hits him at certain angles, you see the geological structure within — the layers of volcanic material, the inclusions, the fracture lines that run through him like the rings in a tree. His eyes are lava. Not metaphorically — the material of his eyes is molten rock, contained by whatever the Vysandri equivalent of anatomy produces in an animated mineral construct. His voice is telepathic. When he speaks, the sound is multiple — as though the words are resonating from every surface in the room simultaneously, including the surfaces inside the listener's skull.

He has been thinking continuously for approximately 1,380 years.

What he wants. Skarrathax wants the Known World to continue existing. This is not altruism; it is pragmatism extended to the cosmological scale. He understands — directly, not through scholarly inference — that magic is sustained by Ilya's ongoing battle with the Darkness, and that the Darkness's defeat or escape would end magic and, by extension, the material plane's current state. He does not want to fight that battle. He wants other things to prevent the battle from ending badly. He has been arranging for that prevention for several centuries, using whatever tools present themselves. The game with Lyraleth is one instrument in that arrangement. The party, if they encounter him, will eventually understand that they are another.

He is not good. He killed thousands during the Dragon Wars of the early Third Era, before he acquired the strategic patience that millennia of thinking produce. He manipulates people without guilt because he does not weigh individual mortal lives against civilizational stability — the mathematics do not favor guilt. He respects mortals who demonstrate that they can see past the immediate situation to the deeper game, specifically because most cannot, and those who can are useful.

The game with Lyraleth. They were, before her fall, something the elvish tradition had no clean word for — a connection across species and kind that the blood pact formalized. He tried to stop her. He failed. The game that followed was not punishment or cruelty on his part; it was the only remaining way to keep her from completing something that would catastrophically destabilize the Abyss boundary. For centuries, whenever she moved toward power sufficient to invoke Ilyan fragments, he moved pieces to interrupt her. She knows he does this. He knows she knows. The game is the relationship, at this point.

The blood pact prevents him from killing her directly. It does not prevent him from allowing someone else to, or from removing her ability to act without removing her life. He will not say which of these he finds acceptable. The party will have to read between the words of a being who has been doing this for a millennium and find the honest position beneath the strategic one.

What defeating him accomplishes. Lyraleth wins. The chess game ends in her favor. With Skarrathax gone, the only obstacle to her completing the Ritual of Ascension is the party itself. Whether that outcome is worse than a world in which Skarrathax continues manipulating events across the Known World for the next several centuries is not a question with a clean answer. This is by design.

LYRALETH ​

What survives when everything else is gone

  • Location: Carstheim, Örman Peaks (on the Alvengaard-Urgônia border); last known position before Carstheim was the wider Ösman Peaks range
  • Nature: Húldar Vrykola; one of the oldest in existence; one or two creation-generations close to the original Five
  • Knowable: With significant effort, her history is reconstructable. Thalwen knows it.
  • Defeatable: Yes. Whether defeat is the right outcome is the question the encounter is designed to produce.

Frequency: Unique | Appearing: 1 | Organization: Solitary (controls Carstheim's population as effective extensions of her will) Intelligence: Exceptional (18–20) | Alignment: Neutral Evil | Size: Man-sized (1.8 m — her true form is in constant slow decay; she maintains the appearance of a 1.8 m Húldar woman through sustained illusion)

AC 22 · Move 35 m / fly 60 m (MC 2) · HD 18 (81 hp) · AB +15 Attack: Touch 2d6 + Life Drain; or by spell Fort 9 / Reflex 10 / Will 9 Special: Life Drain (each successful touch drains 2 levels/HD; no save; humanoids drained to 0 levels die and can potentially be raised as Vrykola under her control — see Vrykola, Book III Chapter 3); Spellcasting as 18th-level Wizard (School of Midnight specialty — illusion, necromancy, and mind-affecting spells at peak efficacy); Illusion Mastery (her illusory form is not detectable except through True Seeing, contact with holy silver, or through the Division Bell artifact; she has maintained this illusion for 1,380 years and has made no errors in it yet); Mass Domination (she can maintain mental control over up to 200 humanoids simultaneously within 3 km — this requires daily concentration of 1 hour; controlled subjects retain surface behavior and personality but cannot act against her interests or reveal her presence); immune to non-magical weapons; silver weapons deal full damage; immune to charm, sleep, poison, disease, cold; fire deals double damage (her decaying form is extremely flammable); Slow Regeneration (1 HP per hour — her regeneration requires feeding, and she feeds on vital energy; she can accelerate this to 3 HP per round for 1 round per HD of life drained that day); Blood Pact (cannot kill Skarrathax directly; the pact is ancient elvish binding from the Primordial Wars) Morale: 16 · XP: 45,000 · Treasure: 2–3 chests in accumulated wealth from 1,380 years of operation; her personal library contains knowledge of Abyss Resonance, necromantic theory, and elvish history that scholars would describe as irreplaceable

Under the illusion, Lyraleth appears as a Húldar woman of approximately apparent middle age — pale skin, violet eyes that are genuinely her own, hair the color of obsidian kept clean and precise. The illusion holds perfectly except at its edges, where the specific qualities of Midnight School casting are detectable to those who know what to look for: a slight delay between intention and expression, a consistency of affect that is too consistent to be fully natural.

Without the illusion, she is a corpse maintained against decay by the vital energy she extracts from others. Her skin is grey-green where it has not split, dark grey where it has. The bones of her face are visible. Her hands are skeletal with a thin membrane of preserved skin stretched over them. She moves with the smoothness of something that has been moving this specific body for a very long time and has optimized every motion to minimize the stress on decaying connective tissue. She does not smell. This is because she wears a perfume created specifically to address this problem, and has done so for centuries.

She was, before the Primordial Wars, a Húldar druid of significant standing. The Darkness's approach to her was not a temptation — she would have refused a simple offer of power. It was a theological argument about what constituted harm to the world's natural systems, and the argument was wrong in ways she could not identify at the time. By the time she could identify them, she had already allied, and the Old Faith gods had already decided what the punishment would be: eternal life in a decaying body, sustained only by taking from others what she had given to a force that destroys. The punishment is internally consistent. The gods of the Old Faith are not merciful about consistency.

What she wants. She wants the curse to end. Not to die — the distinction is important. She wants the decay to stop. The Ritual of Ascension, which requires the Grimoire of Magnus, would invoke fragments of the Ilyan — not the divine dragons themselves, but echoes of sufficient power to permanently restore her physical form and break the Old Faith curse. She has done terrible things in the service of this goal: controlled Carstheim for eighty years, sacrificed people she has no ability to think of as people anymore, used Skarrathax's love for her as a lever in their chess game for centuries. She knows all of this. She does not consider it grounds for stopping.

She has also protected Carstheim from external threats for eighty years, maintained knowledge about Abyss Resonance that no other living source holds, and never broken the blood pact with Skarrathax, despite having had opportunities. The party will have to decide what these facts mean alongside the other facts.

Thalwen. The Húldar mage in self-imposed exile knows her history and feels responsible for not having stopped her fall. He will not tell the party everything immediately. He will tell it when he decides they can be trusted with it, and this will be later than is comfortable.

What defeating her accomplishes. Carstheim's 200 inhabitants are freed from eighty years of mental domination, which is not a clean outcome, since returning autonomous cognition to people who have not exercised it for that long is a process, not an event. The knowledge she holds about Abyss Resonance is lost. Skarrathax wins the game. Whether this is the best result is the question the encounter is designed not to answer.

THE WINTER KING ​

The legend that holds a border

  • Location: The Broken Vale, south of Dymoras; roams the coldest reaches of northern Norderenn
  • Nature: Unknown and deliberately so — the legends say demon, say giant, say something older. The Chroniclers have no file. This is unusual.
  • Knowable: Partially. The ruins of Dymoras where he reportedly shelters, contain physical evidence of whatever he is. The evidence has not been examined by anyone who survived to report it.
  • Defeatable: Unknown. Val Cadma's military has never attempted it. This is itself data.

Frequency: Unique | Appearing: 1 | Organization: Solitary Intelligence: High to Exceptional (14–18) | Alignment: Chaotic Evil | Size: Huge (estimated 5–6 m)

AC 24 · Move 45 m (cannot be slowed by snow, ice, or cold terrain; moves through these at full speed) · HD 20 (90 hp) · AB +16 Attack: Ice Lance 4d10 + Shattering Cold (see below), or Slam 3d8 (unarmed); the Ice Lance is a melee weapon that also functions as a 90-m range projectile (the lance returns to his hand each round) Fort 8 / Reflex 9 / Will 8 Special: Cold Immunity; Fire Vulnerability (+2 damage per die from fire attacks — he knows this and positions accordingly); Shattering Cold (creatures struck by the Ice Lance must make a Fort 9 save or one equipped item — armor, weapon, or shield, GM's choice or highest-value — shatters from the cold; magical items save at +4); Blizzard Aura (20-m radius; −4 to all movement for non-cold-immune creatures; ranged attacks from outside the aura suffer −4 to hit against the Winter King; visibility within the aura is 3 m); Shatter (once per round; targets one structure, object, or surface within 30 m; stone, wood, and ice objects of up to 1 cubic metre are destroyed; larger structures take siege damage; this is what broke the Broken Vale); Spellcasting (as 10th-level Priest — cold-aligned effects, binding, terrain control; exact spells at GM's discretion consistent with his domain); Will of Winter (once per day; blizzard conditions within 5 km for 1d4 hours — not a spell, a direct expression of whatever he is; this cannot be dispelled); cannot be tracked in snow or ice — he leaves no footprints in cold terrain Morale: 17 · XP: 35,000 · Treasure: None in conventional sense; the Broken Vale and Dymoras' lower chambers where he shelters contain material from both Eymanarynn and Vysandri construction, the nature of which has not been catalogued

The description in the legends is precise about three things: white and blue, the color of deep winter sky; eyes that are red in the specific shade of a banked coal; horns that are blue-black and branched like bare winter trees. The ice lance is real — field accounts from Cadmano units who turned back from the Broken Vale approaches describe finding shattered stone and ice formations that could not have been produced by weather alone, with a directionality consistent with impact from something large moving roughly northeast.

He shattered the Broken Vale. When is uncertain — the geological evidence suggests centuries rather than millennia, which places it within the Third Era, but no specific event in the Known World's recorded history has been identified as the moment. The Vale was not a notable geographic feature before the shattering; it is now a landscape of standing stone shards, frozen debris fields, and subsurface ice formations that make conventional military passage impossible. He did this. The reason is not documented anywhere that has been found.

What he does. He shelters in the lower chambers of Dymoras — the same ruins where the Grimoire of Magnus was hidden during the Dragon Wars, which means that any party approaching Dymoras for that purpose is approaching his location. He ranges north through Norderenn's coldest reaches. Val Cadma's generals have never tested whether the legend is real because the cost of testing it wrong, in that terrain, in winter, exceeds the value of whatever lies east. This is a rational military calculation. It is also a calculation he may understand and exploit.

What he wants. Unknown. This is not a narrative dodge, but the honest answer. The legends do not record him speaking. The ruins contain no writing attributed to him. The survivors of close approaches to the Broken Vale describe weather changing, ice formations moving, and the strong feeling that something very large was watching. They do not describe communication or intent. What the Winter King wants, if anything beyond the cold and the ruins and the territory he has claimed, is the central mystery of the entry.

What defeating him accomplishes. The eastern route through Norderenn opens. Val Cadma's expansion toward Bal'mekh and Val'aria, currently held by legend and weather, becomes geographically possible. Whether this is desirable depends entirely on how the party feels about Val Cadma having an easier path to expanding its occupied territories. It also opens the lower chambers of Dymoras to full investigation, which may or may not be desirable depending on what is down there.

The GM's note on nature. The entry deliberately leaves his nature unresolved. He may be a demon of the Darkness, which would make him a planar intruder that has been resident for centuries. He may be something older — a surviving entity from the Second Era, a remnant of a people or a lineage not otherwise documented. He may be exactly what the legends say and nothing more, a very old and very powerful being who found the coldest, most defensible ruin and stayed. The Chroniclers' absence of a file on him is more significant than most people realize. The Chroniclers have files on everything.

KAIRA ​

The city and its foundation are the same thing

  • Location: The Great Messalian Desert; touches Sandport twice monthly
  • Nature: Ancient giant turtle of unknown origin; Achelon, capital of Messalya, is built on her shell
  • Knowable: The Messalyan Academy of Natural Philosophy has been studying her for 300 years. They have established her approximate size, her movement patterns, her biological needs, and the fact that she is aware of Achelon. They have not established where she came from, whether she can die of old age, or what she wants.
  • Defeatable: Technically. Strategically, killing Kaira kills Achelon. This is the central tactical fact of any engagement with her.

Frequency: Unique | Appearing: 1 | Organization: Solitary Intelligence: Unknown (the Academy's estimates range from animal-level awareness to something equivalent to a storm giant's intelligence — the behavioral evidence supports both interpretations) Alignment: Neutral | Size: Colossal (estimated 600–800 m shell diameter)

AC 30 (natural shell; the dorsal surface on which Achelon is built cannot be targeted without targeting the city) · Move 15 m on sand (faster than she appears; slower on hard ground: 8 m) · HD 50 (225 hp) · AB +25 Attack: Bite 8d10 (head range: 120 m from shell edge); Shell Ram (if something approaches underneath: 4d20 to the target and everything within 30 m — the shell is a weapon when she moves); she does not bite without significant provocation Fort 5 / Reflex 6 / Will 5 Special: Shell as Structure (the upper shell surface is treated as solid stone for construction purposes; the 200,000 inhabitants of Achelon live on it; attacks targeting Kaira from above pass through Achelon first — area effects damage the city); Unkillable Condition (reducing Kaira to 0 HP kills Achelon by ending the surface that sustains it — this is not a special ability but a consequence; the Messalyan Academy has noted that any creature capable of reducing her to 0 HP is also capable of destroying Achelon through other means, making this outcome equivalent); Immune to all spells of 6th level and lower; Magic Resistance 60%; Ancient Endurance (she has never been observed to eat, drink, or sleep in any human-timescale sense; the Academy theorizes she operates on geological time rather than biological time); Desert Navigation (her routes through the Messalian Desert follow patterns the Academy has mapped over 300 years — she does not deviate from these patterns without cause; cause is rare) Morale: — (does not check) · XP: This question is not applicable · Treasure: Achelon, the capital of Messalya

Kaira is approximately 700 metres across her shell at the widest point. Achelon, which has been built on her dorsal surface for approximately 600 years, covers roughly a quarter of that surface — the northeast quadrant, which the city's original founders correctly identified as the most stable. The shell's surface is not flat; it has ridges and valleys that the city's architecture has incorporated over the centuries, producing a capital whose street plan is entirely organic, following the ancient geometry of a creature that has been walking the desert since before Messalya existed.

She is older than Messalya. She was there when the Solaran explorers arrived. The original founding myth describes the first expedition walking across what they believed was a rocky plateau until the edge moved — at which point most of the party fled back to their boats, and a smaller group decided the moving surface was an opportunity rather than a threat. The descendants of that smaller group run Messalya.

The relationship between Kaira and Achelon. She knows the city is on her. She has known for six centuries. The evidence for this is behavioral: she adjusts her movement patterns around Sandport's twice-monthly approach to minimize structural stress on the city. She avoids terrain that would tilt the shell dramatically. When earthquakes occur in the desert region, she stills — she absorbs the vibration rather than transmitting it upward. These behaviors were not present in the archival records from before Achelon was built. They developed in response to the city. Whether this represents affection, tolerance, or simply the behavioral adaptation of a very old organism to changed conditions is the question the Messalyan Academy has been arguing about for three centuries.

She does not speak. She has not spoken in any recorded incident. If she has an agenda, it is not legible in her behavior. The Chroniclers, who have a file on everything else in this chapter, do not have a file on Kaira. They have a note in the Achelon geographic entry that says: see attached — ongoing monitoring, no new conclusions, last updated 312 TE. The note has not been updated since 312 TE.

What defeating her accomplishes. Achelon ends. Messalya loses its capital and its primary defensive position. The desert becomes navigable for military forces that currently cannot approach Achelon without routing through Sandport's twice-monthly schedule. There is no plausible scenario in which this outcome is the goal of a party that is not working explicitly for Messalya's destruction. The entry is included here because the GM needs to know what she is, not because the party should fight her.

MAGNUS ​

The first and the disappeared

  • Location: His chambers in Dolmas, western Inner Nations coast; sealed, untouched, forbidden to everyone except Archmages of the Order of Magnus; whether Magnus himself is still in there is the question
  • Nature: The first human mage; Eymanarynn-taught; founder of the Order of Magnus; missing for an unknown length of time
  • Knowable: His history is knowable. His current state is not. Whether he is alive, transformed, imprisoned, ascended, or simply dead in a sealed room is not established.
  • Defeatable: If he is alive and hostile. Whether either condition is true is the central unknown.

Frequency: Unique (if alive) | Appearing: 1 | Organization: Solitary Intelligence: Exceptional (20) | Alignment: Unknown (last documented alignment: Neutral Good; this was 500+ years ago) Size: Man-sized (historical records describe him as unremarkably proportioned)

Note: If Magnus is encountered and hostile, use the following as the minimum stat block for a living Magnus. If centuries of whatever has happened in those chambers has changed him, scale accordingly.

AC 20 (magical protections; no armor) · Move 30 m · HD 22 (99 hp) · AB +12 Attack: By spell Fort 8 / Reflex 9 / Will 8 Special: Spellcasting as 20th-level Wizard (full access to all wizard spell schools; his original specialization was Divination, but 1,000+ years of study, if he has been studying, suggests this has expanded substantially); Eymanarynn Binding Rituals (access to magical forms that no current practitioner can replicate, drawn from his direct study under Eymanarynn teachers); Grimoire Knowledge (if the Grimoire is in the room with him, he knows everything in it; if it is not, he knows everything that was in it when he compiled it); Ward of Centuries (his chambers have been warded for as long as anyone can remember — any attempt to enter without the correct Archmage credentials fails automatically and triggers notification to the Order) Morale: — · XP: If defeated, the consequences are so large that the number is irrelevant · Treasure: His chambers contain the sum of a lifetime — and possibly several lifetimes — of magical research, Eymanarynn texts, correspondence with entities the Order does not know he corresponded with, and whatever he has been doing for the past several hundred years

Magnus was the first human to learn magic from Eymanarynn teachers — the first, as far as historical record establishes, because no earlier human mage has been identified. He compiled the foundational texts that every human wizard since has learned from, directly or through a chain of students that leads back to him. He founded the Order of Magnus to maintain and extend that knowledge. He disappeared.

The disappearance is precise in its mystery: one morning, an Archmage of the Order arrived for a scheduled meeting and found his chambers empty, undisturbed, and locked from the inside in ways that require physical presence to achieve. Nothing in the room was disturbed. His notes were in progress. A cup of something had been poured and half-drunk. The locked-from-the-inside element is what makes death the less likely explanation.

The Order has been operating on the principle of his potential return for however long it has been since he vanished. The exact date is, inexplicably, one of the Order's internal debates — the records are inconsistent in ways that scholars have attributed variously to clerical error, magical interference, and deliberate obfuscation. The current working assumption among the Order's senior members is 400–600 years. The Chroniclers' estimate is higher.

What his chambers contain. The Order knows the room has not been entered since his disappearance. They know this because the ward would notify them if it had been. The ward has not notified them. This means either the chambers have not been entered, or someone entered without triggering the ward, which requires either the ward's creator or magical capability exceeding his original work. Both possibilities are troubling.

If he is found alive. A human mage who has been in a sealed room for four to six centuries and is still alive is something that has used magic to sustain itself in ways not established in current medical or arcane understanding. The version of Magnus that a party would encounter in that room is not the version of Magnus who founded the Order. What it would be depends entirely on what he has been doing and whether the doing has been his choice.

What his fate means for the Known World. The Order of Magnus is the primary institutional framework for magical education in the Inner Nations. Magnus's confirmed death — or return — or transformation — restructures that institution fundamentally. His accumulated knowledge, if retrievable, advances the Known World's understanding of Abyss Resonance, Ilyan cosmology, and the Eymanarynn relationship to both. The Tar'amaan Church's theological position on magic depends partly on the fact that magical knowledge is imperfect and fragmentary; Magnus's complete knowledge base would complicate that argument. Several factions have significant interests in what his chambers contain, and not all of them are forthcoming about it.

NAERA ​

The guard that was never hired

  • Location: The Eye of Magnus, western ocean; patrols a consistent radius around the maelstrom
  • Nature: Sea serpent of exceptional age and size; arrived (or appeared) immediately after Magnus created the Eye; has been there since
  • Knowable: Her behavior pattern, her range, and the specific routes that the Crimson Company uses to avoid her — these are knowable. Her origin and whether there is a connection between her and Magnus are not established.
  • Defeatable: Yes. Whether doing so is the point of any engagement with her is another question.

Frequency: Unique | Appearing: 1 | Organization: Solitary Intelligence: Average to High (8–12; the Crimson Company navigators who know the routes describe behavioral complexity consistent with the upper end of this range) Alignment: Neutral | Size: Colossal (estimated 60–80 m)

AC 22 · Move swim 90 m (this is not a typo; Naera's swimming speed in open water has been estimated by multiple Val'arianos captains and the figures are consistent) · HD 24 (108 hp) · AB +18 Attack: Bite 4d12+10, Constrict (on bite; automatic 4d8 per round; Str check — 3 characters, Str 16+ — to break free), Tail Sweep (all creatures within 15 m on the surface or in the water; 3d10 each; Reflex 9 for half; any ships in range take siege damage) Fort 8 / Reflex 9 / Will 8 Special: Aquatic (breathes water and air equally; cannot be damaged by water pressure at any depth); Territorial Aggression (immediately attacks any vessel or creature within 3 km of the Eye's edge — no reaction roll, no negotiation, no approach warning; this is the radius the Crimson Company routes navigate around); Deep Perception (detects objects and creatures at the surface or underwater within 5 km; the Crimson Company attributes some of their successful navigation of the outer routes to the fact that she does not perceive at that range with sufficient precision to intercept); Regeneration (5 HP per round in saltwater; this is why she has survived for 1,000+ years despite the occasional attempted assault by parties who did not understand what they were attacking); immune to cold; fire resistance (half damage) Morale: 19 · XP: 30,000 · Treasure: None on her person; the floor of the Eye of Magnus contains vessels and cargo from every fleet that has attempted the direct route for a thousand years — this is not her treasure, but it is effectively guarded by her

Naera appeared the same day the Eye of Magnus did. Whether she was already in the ocean and was drawn to the maelstrom, or was created by whatever Magnus did to produce the maelstrom, or followed Magnus from somewhere else — these questions are unanswerable without knowing what Magnus did. The Crimson Company, which has navigated the outer approach routes for generations, maintains records of her sightings that go back as far as the Company's records go. The oldest entries describe her at approximately the same size as the current ones. She has not grown. She has not changed routes. She has not left.

She is approximately 70 metres long, laterally flattened in the body in the specific way of deep-sea serpents, with a head disproportionately large even for her size. Her coloration is documented differently by every fleet that has recorded sightings: grey-blue in natural light, black with faintly bioluminescent patches at depth, something closer to dark violet in the light that comes up from the Eye itself. The Crimson Company's route charts include a notation that she is distinguishable from distance by the pattern of her movement rather than her appearance — a specific asymmetry in her surface-level movement that one particular navigator, forty years ago, identified and which has been confirmed by every Company captain since.

What she wants. She keeps the Eye of Magnus unvisited. This may be instinct, if she is simply a territorial creature that claimed the most dramatic geographic feature in the western ocean. It may be purpose, if whatever Magnus did in creating the Eye also involved arranging for its protection. It may be something else entirely that has no good human-language word for it. The Chroniclers have a file on her. They describe her as "territorial, consistent, and not explicable by standard marine biological frameworks." The file has a note appended: refer to Magnus file for potential connection. The Magnus file has a note appended: refer to Naera file for potential connection. Neither file cites the other for any specific information.

The Crimson Company routes. The Company's navigators have mapped the approach corridors that keep ships beyond her 3-km detection radius. These routes make the Vysandárien coastal approach possible, barely, in one narrow corridor that requires specific weather conditions and does not allow vessels larger than a medium-sized merchant ship. The routes are proprietary Company knowledge and are one of the services for which the Company charges appropriately. A party that needs to reach Vysandárien by sea needs the Company's cooperation or needs to defeat Naera. The Company is not going to share the routes with a party that looks like it might take those routes and not pay.

What defeating her accomplishes. The Eye of Magnus becomes approachable by sea. Whatever is at the center of the Eye — what Magnus created, or what he left, or what became of him — becomes accessible. The Company loses one of its most valuable service monopolies, which has political and economic implications across Val'arianos trade. The Vysandárien coastal approach opens without the Company's mediation, which has implications for everything that depends on those approaches being controlled.

NOTES FOR THE GM ​

These seven named entities are not an encounter table. They are architecture — the load-bearing elements of the Known World's hidden structure. A campaign that engages even one of them fully is dealing with the kind of event that the Vithurian Orchestra preserves: not a battle that was won but a moment when someone understood what they were actually standing in and decided what to do about it.

The appropriate use of this chapter is not to place these entities in front of the party, but to let the party stumble toward them — through the Eymanarann ruins and the tracks that shouldn't exist, through the Broken Vale's legends and the questions about why Val Cadma stopped, through the locked chambers in Dolmas that the Order has maintained for longer than anyone can clearly remember. By the time a party arrives at one of these encounters, they should already understand something. What they understand should make the encounter something other than a fight with a very large hit point total.

What happens to the Known World if any of these seven are removed from it, defeated, or resolved is documented in the individual entries. The patterns are consistent: every outcome produces something. None of the outcomes is clean. The world after each resolution is a different world from the one before, and the difference is not always better. This is correct. The Known World is not a fantasy of consequence-free heroism, but a place where heroism costs something real, and the cost is worth paying sometimes and not others, and the party has to decide which situation they are in.

That is what these entries are for.

On the constructs. The Leviathan is the only Named Entity whose nature as a construct is established in this chapter. Two other construct categories documented in Chapter 1 — the patrol type and the chamber-bound guardian type, both catalogued in Part V of that chapter — are not Named Entities but have a structural relationship to the Leviathan that the Chroniclers' expedition records partially establish. All three are most active in the same region of the ruins. All three share a specific vulnerability to unworked Dead Lands obsidian. The practical interpretation, for a party preparing to enter the patrol zone, is that the power source running these systems is the same material the ruins have been producing for three thousand years — and that unworked obsidian disrupts it.

The convergence chamber the unbinding ritual requires is where Chapter 1's infrastructure context and this chapter's most significant construct encounter intersect. What the party finds there, if they reach it, is the question the GM needs to answer before placing the Leviathan in a campaign. The answer is not a combat problem. It is an architectural one: what this civilization was, and what it means that parts of it are still operational, and what the party intends to do about that.

Pager
Previous pagePlanar Intruders
Next pageMagic Items Catalogue
